frameworks.name: Four Components of Equity Framework
frameworks.definition: A decomposition of what real equity provides into four distinct elements — cash flow, sale bonus, risk, and control — used to identify which elements a prospective equity recipient actually wants, then substitute non-dilutive instruments for those elements only.

frameworks.name: Profit Share Pool Structure
frameworks.definition: A fixed percentage of annual profit (typically 10–20%) set aside as a leadership incentive pool; individual leaders receive slices of that pool rather than ownership stakes, capping total incentive spend while creating owner-like thinking.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that founders who do not intend to sell their business should never give away real equity shares to key employees or leaders, because the only thing guaranteed over a 10-year horizon is that the founder will still be present — all other outcomes (employee tenure, business trajectory) are uncertain, making equity grants a permanent cost for an uncertain benefit.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Founder has no near-term intention to sell the business
claims.mechanism: Equity is irrevocable once granted; if the employee leaves or underperforms, the founder cannot recover the ownership stake, making the expected cost high relative to uncertain retention benefit.
bounded_by: Does not apply when a sale is actively planned and phantom equity at exit is insufficient motivation
Does not apply to co-founders who have taken on comparable risk from day one

claims.text: Alex Hormozi identifies four and only four things that real equity provides to a recipient: (1) ongoing cash flow from profits, (2) a sale bonus at exit, (3) exposure to downside risk, and (4) voting control over business decisions. He uses this decomposition to show that most employees want only items 1 and 2, which can be replicated with profit-share and a contractual sale bonus without granting ownership.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
bounded_by: Employees who want control or want to bear downside risk cannot be satisfied by non-dilutive substitutes

claims.text: Alex Hormozi recommends a profit-share pool of 10–20% of annual profit as the primary leadership incentive structure for scaling companies; individual leaders receive slices of this fixed pool, which caps total incentive spend while creating ownership thinking because leaders must evaluate new hires the same way an owner does — each addition dilutes the existing slice but should grow the total pool.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: Leaders who receive slices of a shared pool internalize the trade-off between adding headcount (diluting their slice) and growing profit (expanding the pool), replicating the owner's economic logic without equity transfer.

numbers.axis: profit share pool as percentage of annual profit

anchors.value: 20% of $100/year profit = $20 always allocated to leaders, founder retains $80

bounded_by: Does not work if the business has no profit (pre-profitability stage)
Does not provide capital-gains tax treatment; distributions are taxed as income in the US

claims.text: Alex Hormozi advises that when designing a profit-share pool, founders should reserve approximately two-thirds of the total leadership pool for future hires rather than allocating it all to current leaders, because — in his framing — the best talent is in the future, not in the present cohort.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: leave probably two-thirds for future talent
conditions: Founder intends to grow to significantly larger headcount
Pool is being set at inception rather than revised periodically
claims.mechanism: Over-allocating the pool at formation leaves no room to attract high-caliber future hires without either diluting current leaders below their expected slice or expanding the pool percentage, which reduces founder take.

numbers.axis: fraction of leadership incentive pool reserved for future hires vs. current leaders

bounded_by: May not apply if the current team is already at its intended final size

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that the practical non-dilutive substitute for equity is: (1) a profit share that comes off the bottom line during employment, and (2) a contractual sale bonus paid in the event of an exit — with a clawback clause stipulating that if the employee leaves before a sale, they receive neither. This structure replicates the cash-flow and exit-upside components of equity without transferring ownership.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: profit share... and if we sell you get money tomorrow
conditions: Employee does not want to take on downside risk
Employee does not require voting control
bounded_by: In the US, a sale bonus is taxed as ordinary income rather than capital gains, making it less tax-efficient than actual equity for the recipient
Legal structures differ by jurisdiction; Hormozi explicitly notes uncertainty about Australian law

claims.text: Alex Hormozi advises that when a sale-bonus clause is included, it should be written as a percentage of cash proceeds actually received by the seller, not as a percentage of the total enterprise valuation agreed in the deal, because valuation and cash received can diverge significantly (e.g., seller financing, earnouts, escrow holds).
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: percentage of cash not calculated on the total value of the sale
conditions: A sale-bonus clause is included in a leadership incentive agreement
claims.mechanism: Enterprise value at signing may include earnouts, seller-financed notes, or equity rollovers that are never realized as cash; tying the bonus to total valuation could force a payout before the founder receives the cash to fund it.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi considers giving equity to a sales-role employee a day-one mistake, on the grounds that a sales hire is not a co-founder and is compensated via commission, which already aligns their incentive with revenue; adding equity on top front-loads the founder's cost before the hire has proven long-term strategic value.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: giving equity to a sales guy is kind of like one of those day one mistakes
conditions: The person is a hired sales employee, not a founder
The employee already receives commission on closed deals
claims.mechanism: Commission already creates revenue alignment; equity adds permanent ownership dilution without adding a capability the founder lacks, which is the threshold test for granting equity.
bounded_by: Does not apply if the sales person is simultaneously building a non-sales function (e.g., full head-of-growth role) and the equity is tied to that broader contribution

claims.text: Alex Hormozi recommends that, rather than granting equity to an ambitious non-founder employee to signal trust, founders should offer a clear, quantifiable performance path to the leadership table: define the specific objective (e.g., build and operate a demand-generation system producing a target number of qualified deals per month), and promise the promotion and associated incentives contingent on achieving that objective.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Employee is ambitious and high-potential but has not yet demonstrated leadership-tier output
Founder wants to retain the employee long-term without premature equity grant
claims.mechanism: A defined, measurable milestone replaces subjective judgment about readiness, prevents equity from being used as a retention bribe before value is proven, and gives the employee a concrete agency path rather than a vague promise.
bounded_by: Requires the founder to actually promote and reward once the milestone is met, or trust is destroyed

claims.text: Alex Hormozi observes that real equity grants to employees are complicated by at least three frictions that profit-share avoids: (1) the need to agree on a business valuation, (2) the requirement that the employee pay for the shares (or the founder pay gift taxes), and (3) the risk that the employee bears ongoing downside. He concludes that most employees are unwilling to accept all three frictions simultaneously, making profit-share the path of least resistance.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: they have to buy it and give you money... most people don't want to do either of those things
conditions: Employee is a key hire in an existing profitable business, not a co-founder in a pre-revenue company
bounded_by: In startup contexts where no valuation exists and risk is shared from day one, traditional equity grants remain conventional

claims.text: Alex Hormozi notes that a profits interest tied to a sale event (a form of phantom equity) can be structured so that the employee's interest is proportional to their share of the profit pool at the time of exit — for example, if the pool is 10% of the company and the employee holds 10% of the pool, they receive 1% of cash proceeds — and that this interest reverts to the founder if the employee departs before a sale.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: profits interest in the sale... proportional to their interest... if they leave it comes back to you
conditions: Founder anticipates a potential sale in the future
Employee is at a leadership level where exit alignment matters
claims.mechanism: Vesting the sale bonus on continued employment at time of sale creates retention leverage; the clawback eliminates the founder's obligation to pay a departing employee for a value-creation event they did not stay to contribute to.

numbers.axis: employee's share of sale proceeds as a function of pool allocation

anchors.value: 1% of cash proceeds received by seller

bounded_by: Tax treatment differs by jurisdiction; in the US a sale bonus is ordinary income, not capital gains
Legal enforceability of clawback clauses varies by jurisdiction

examples.text: During a live Q&A session, a founder describes having two leadership hires — a head of growth and a head of strategy — both currently on salary and asking about an equity incentive plan. Alex Hormozi immediately recommends a profit-share pool of 10–20% as the default answer, illustrating the math with a $100/year profit example: 20% ($20) goes to the leadership pool, leaving the founder with $80. He walks through how leaders receiving pool slices will naturally think like owners when considering new hires, because each addition dilutes their own slice while (ideally) growing the total pool.

Hormozi shares a personal cautionary note about sale-bonus clause drafting: he recounts being personally harmed by a clause written against total enterprise valuation rather than actual cash received, and explicitly flags this as a mistake to avoid — establishing that the distinction between valuation and cash proceeds is not theoretical but has material financial consequences.

A founder describes their head-of-growth hire as their first non-founder sales leader, who is currently on commission and is being considered for equity as part of a leadership incentive plan. Hormozi responds that giving equity to a sales employee is a day-one mistake, then pivots to prescribe an alternative: rather than equity, the founder should set a quantifiable milestone (e.g., consistently generating five qualified deals per month through a demand-generation system he builds) and offer the leadership-table incentives only after that milestone is met.

practices.text: When writing a leadership incentive agreement that includes a sale bonus, specify the payout as a percentage of actual cash received by the seller at closing, not as a percentage of the total agreed enterprise value, to prevent situations where the founder owes a bonus before receiving the corresponding cash.
Before allocating any profit-share pool to current leaders, reserve approximately two-thirds of the pool for future hires who will be needed as the business scales.

When an ambitious non-founder employee asks about equity, replace the equity conversation with a documented, quantifiable performance milestone that unlocks the leadership table and associated incentives — do not use equity as a retention bribe before the milestone is earned.

Structure any sale-contingent incentive so that the employee's interest reverts to the founder if the employee leaves before a sale occurs, to prevent paying out for a value-creation event the departing employee did not contribute to.
